Our first blast of high heat and humidity settles in this week.
An Extreme Heat Warning for Greater Cincinnati lasts through Thursday at 8 p.m.
Afternoons will be dangerously hot for spending extended periods of time outside. Take breaks when possible and drink plenty of water.
We don't get much relief from the heat during the overnights and mornings, as lows only reach the mid-70s most of this week.
According to Hamilton County health officials, when temperatures reach the upper 90s and into the 100s for even a short period of time, residents need to take extra precautions with their health.
The most common problems associated with extreme heat are heat exhaustion and heat stroke.
According to health officials, heat exhaustion or heat stress is the overheating of the body due to excessive loss of water. It can be caused by prolonged exposure to hot temperatures, limited fluid and/or insufficient dietary salt intake.
What are the warning signs?
If left untreated, it could progress to heat stroke so residents are asked to see a doctor if symptoms last longer than an hour.
According to health officials, heat stroke, as opposed to heat exhaustion, can be fatal.
What are the symptoms of heat stroke?
Symptoms in addition to those for heat exhaustion include:
Health officials are offering tips to prevent both heat exhaustion and heat stroke:
- Plan outdoor activities in the early morning or late afternoon.
- If you feel lightheaded or dizzy stop what you are doing and go inside.
- Apply sunscreen. Skin cannot sweat through sunburn.
- Wear light-colored, loose-fitting clothes and hats that allow airflow.
- Drink plenty of water. Don’t wait until you’re thirsty – your body may already be dehydrating.
- Avoid food and beverages that promote fluid loss, such as caffeine or alcohol.
- Never leave anybody, especially children, unattended in a locked car during hot weather. Car temperatures rise rapidly and can be deadly in minutes.
- Monitor those at risk such as elderly relatives and neighbors or people who do not have air conditioning.

title: As temperatures climb, health experts say GLP-1 users face higher dehydration risk - wvtm
search_term: (heatstroke OR "heat stroke" OR sunstroke OR "heat exhaustion" OR "heat-related death" OR "heat casualty" OR "heat fatality" OR "suspected heat death" OR dehydration OR "heat cramps" OR "heat illness") Assam

The hot weather is sticking around this week, and health experts say it's important to take extra precautions if you're spending time outside, especially if you're taking a GLP-1 weight-loss medication.On July 1st, a new Medicare program starts that allows people to get GLP-1 for 50 dollars a month.With more people expected to be taking weight-loss medications, medical professionals are emphasizing the importance of staying hydrated, limiting time in the sun, and watching for signs of heat-related illness.Ivana Posey, a nurse practitioner at Infinity Meadows Spa, says people taking GLP-1 medications should be especially mindful of their water intake."Water intake helps us to actually flush out our kidneys and helps us to flush out what we're actually losing when we're doing weight loss and GLP-1s; they can dehydrate us. So we really need to stress water intake so that we're not getting dehydrated on top of the heat," said Posey.She explained that lack of hydration can increase heat stroke, and people on GLP-1's need more hydration than others."Water intake is extremely important. At least 64oz or more a day is super, super key to any weight loss journey. And then on top of that, in the summertime, we want you to be closer to 80oz," said Posey.Precautions everyone can take this summer to stay safe:Take breaks from the sunWear a wet, cool rag on neck if doing yardworkWear long-sleeve shirtsReplace your electrolytes as you sweat
BIRMINGHAM, Ala. — The hot weather is sticking around this week, and health experts say it's important to take extra precautions if you're spending time outside, especially if you're taking a GLP-1 weight-loss medication.
On July 1st, a new Medicare program starts that allows people to get GLP-1 for 50 dollars a month.
With more people expected to be taking weight-loss medications, medical professionals are emphasizing the importance of staying hydrated, limiting time in the sun, and watching for signs of heat-related illness.
Ivana Posey, a nurse practitioner at Infinity Meadows Spa, says people taking GLP-1 medications should be especially mindful of their water intake.
"Water intake helps us to actually flush out our kidneys and helps us to flush out what we're actually losing when we're doing weight loss and GLP-1s; they can dehydrate us. So we really need to stress water intake so that we're not getting dehydrated on top of the heat," said Posey.
She explained that lack of hydration can increase heat stroke, and people on GLP-1's need more hydration than others.
"Water intake is extremely important. At least 64oz or more a day is super, super key to any weight loss journey. And then on top of that, in the summertime, we want you to be closer to 80oz," said Posey.
Precautions everyone can take this summer to stay safe:
- Replace your electrolytes as you sweat

title: City Offers Cooling Center at Kirksey Recreation Center - Livonia.gov
search_term: (heatstroke OR "heat stroke" OR sunstroke OR "heat exhaustion" OR "heat-related death" OR "heat casualty" OR "heat fatality" OR "suspected heat death" OR dehydration OR "heat cramps" OR "heat illness") Assam
full_text:
The National Weather Service has issued a Heat Advisory from noon Tuesday, June 30, to 8 p.m. Thursday, July 2. The City of Livonia hosts a cooling center at the Kirksey Recreation Center and Livonia Senior Wellness Center for residents looking for refuge from the heat on those days during operating hours.
Kirksey Recreation Center Hours:
- Monday-Friday: 5 a.m. to 10 p.m.
Saturday: 6 a.m. to 7 p.m.
- Sunday: 7 a.m. to 7 p.m.
Livonia Senior Wellness Center Hours:
- Monday-Friday: 8:30 a.m. to 4:45 p.m.
NOTE: The LSWC is closed Friday, July 3
The Rec and Senior Wellness Center have facilities that can provide short-term heat relief for those who may need it. There are also WiFi access and televisions available, along with plugs for cell phone chargers.
We encourage everyone to use caution when we experience high temperatures. Below are some tips to stay safe:
- Drink plenty of fluids to stay hydrated.
- Stay out of direct sunlight, if possible. Bring pets indoors and check on others.
- Avoid high-energy activity or outdoor work during midday heat, if possible.
- Check on family, seniors and neighbors.
- Know the signs of heat sickness and watch for heat cramps, heat exhaustion and heat stroke.

title: Heat Safety Guide: What to know about heat stroke, heat exhaustion, and other heat-related illnesses - WXII

Summer is upon us, and with it comes the concern for heat-related illnesses.
Did you know that heat kills? An average of 658 people die each year from heat-related illnesses, according to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention.
What happens to the body during excessive heat?
►Get the latest forecast and heat information
When heat and humidity occur, it makes it really difficult for your body’s ability to cool itself. If your body heats too rapidly, it doesn’t give your body proper time to cool itself, according to the CDC. Your body can also lose too much fluid or salt through dehydration or sweating. If your body temperature rises, you could suffer from a heat-related illness.
That’s why it’s so important to know the symptoms of heat stroke, heat exhaustion, Rhabdomyolysis, heat syncope, heat cramps and heat rash.
►Get the latest list of cooling centers opening.
All information below is provided by the CDC.
Heat Safety
Heat Stroke
Heat stroke is the most serious heat-related illness. It occurs when the body can no longer control its temperature: the body’s temperature rises rapidly, the sweating mechanism fails, and the body is unable to cool down. When heat stroke occurs, the body temperature can rise to 106°F or higher within 10 to 15 minutes. Heat stroke can cause permanent disability or death if the person does not receive emergency treatment.
Symptoms
Symptoms of heat stroke include:
First Aid
Take the following steps to treat a worker with heat stroke:
- Call 911 for emergency medical care.
- Stay with the worker until emergency medical services arrive.
- Move the worker to a shaded, cool area and remove outer clothing.
- Cool the worker quickly, using the following methods:
- Circulate the air around the worker to speed cooling.
- Place cold wet cloths or ice on the head, neck, armpits, and groin; or soak the clothing with cool water.
Heat Exhaustion
Heat exhaustion is the body’s response to an excessive loss of water and salt, usually through excessive sweating. Heat exhaustion is most likely to affect:
Symptoms
Symptoms of heat exhaustion include:
First Aid
Treat a worker who has heat exhaustion by doing the following:
- Take worker to a clinic or emergency room for medical evaluation and treatment.
- Call 911 if medical care is unavailable.
- Have someone stay with the worker until help arrives.
- Remove the worker from the hot area and give liquids to drink.
- Remove unnecessary clothing, including shoes and socks.
- Cool the worker with cold compresses or have the worker wash their head, face, and neck with cold water.
- Encourage frequent sips of cool water.
Get the latest news stories of interest by clicking here
Rhabdomyolysis
Rhabdomyolysis (rhabdo) is a medical condition associated with heat stress and prolonged physical exertion. Rhabdo causes the rapid breakdown, rupture, and death of muscle. When muscle tissue dies, electrolytes and large proteins are released into the bloodstream. This can cause irregular heart rhythms, seizures, and damage to the kidneys.
Symptoms
Symptoms of rhabdo include:
First Aid
Workers with symptoms of rhabdo should:
- Seek immediate care at the nearest medical facility.
- Ask to be checked for rhabdomyolysis (i.e., blood sample analyzed for creatine kinase).
To learn more, visit NIOSH Rhabdomyolysis.
Heat Syncope
Heat syncope is a fainting (syncope) episode or dizziness that usually occurs when standing for too long or suddenly standing up after sitting or lying. Factors that may contribute to heat syncope include dehydration and lack of acclimatization.
Symptoms
Symptoms of heat syncope include:
- Light-headedness from standing too long or suddenly rising from a sitting or lying position
First Aid
Workers with heat syncope should:
- Sit or lie down in a cool place.
- Slowly drink water, clear juice, or a sports drink.
Heat Cramps
Heat cramps usually affect workers who sweat a lot during strenuous activity. This sweating depletes the body’s salt and moisture levels. Low salt levels in muscles cause painful cramps. Heat cramps may also be a symptom of heat exhaustion.
Symptoms
Muscle cramps, pain, or spasms in the abdomen, arms, or legs
First Aid
Workers with heat cramps should do the following:
- Drink water and have a snack or a drink that replaces carbohydrates and electrolytes (such as sports drinks) every 15 to 20 minutes.
Heat Rash
Heat rash is a skin irritation caused by excessive sweating during hot, humid weather.
Symptoms
Symptoms of heat rash include:
- Red clusters of pimples or small blisters
- Usually appears on the neck, upper chest, groin, under the breasts, and in elbow creases
First Aid
Workers who have heat rash should:
- Work in a cooler, less humid environment, if possible.

As the southwest monsoon has advanced across most parts of the country and brought relief from extreme heat, there is forecast of heavy to very heavy rainfall in Mumbai over the next few days. The Indian Meteorological Department has issued an orange alert and advised people to remain cautious.
Thane, Palgarh, Mumbai, Raigarh will observe heavy to very heavy rainfall. A few places in the city will observe extremely heavy rainfall from July 3, according to the forecast.
Monsoon arrived over a week late than its usual June 11 onset in Mumbai, but with southwest monsoon now covering entire Maharashtra, widespread rainfall is expected across the city.
The day temperature is likely to hover around 29 degrees Celsius, while the minimum temperature is expected to settle close to 23 degrees Celsius.
Southwest monsoon advances further
Trending Stories
Meanwhile, the IMD in its weather bulletin on Tuesday said that the southwest monsoon has advanced across most parts of the country.
Northeast India, Sub-Himalayan West Bengal, Sikkim, Bihar and Odisha are expected to witness widespread rainfall. Maharashtra, Madhya Pradesh and Chhattisgarh in Western and Central India may receive heavy rainfall as monsoon activity strengthen.
Other places to receive good amounts of rain in the next few days are Kerala, Coastal Karnataka, Konkan and Goa and coastal Gujarat.
The hill states of Himachal and Uttarakhand will also witness good amounts of rain with the progresses of monsoon line.
The progress of monsoon and weather systems indicate that rainfall activity is likely to improve during July, said IMD. Better rainfall in the core monsoon months of July and August could reduce the seasonal deficit if weather systems remain favourable.

title: How to spot the difference between heat exhaustion and heat stroke - Milwaukee Journal Sentinel
search_term: (heatstroke OR "heat stroke" OR sunstroke OR "heat exhaustion" OR "heat-related death" OR "heat casualty" OR "heat fatality" OR "suspected heat death" OR dehydration OR "heat cramps" OR "heat illness") Assam
full_text:
As the temperature rises, the risk of heat exhaustion and heat stroke increases. And while both illnesses have a similar cause, they can lead to drastically different outcomes.
Here's what to know about heat exhaustion and heat stroke.
What is the difference between heat exhaustion and heat stroke?
In short, heat stroke is more serious than heat exhaustion.
“People often talk about heat exhaustion and heat stroke as though they are separate things," Dr. Thomas Waters said in a Cleveland Clinic heat illness guide. "But they exist on a spectrum from not-so-serious to a significant and life-threatening emergency."
The Mayo Clinic defines heat exhaustion as a condition that happens when your body overheats, often accompanied by heavy sweating and a rapid pulse. Anyone suffering from heat exhaustion should cool off and drink fluids, and if symptoms don't improve or get worse within an hour, consult a doctor.
Heat stroke, though, is the most severe form of heat illness, with symptoms that include confusion, altered mental state and a body temperature above 104 degrees, according to the Mayo Clinic. If you notice signs of heat stroke, call 911 and seek medical attention right away.
“Heat stroke is an emergency,” Waters said. “It can become deadly very quickly. Heat stroke isn’t something you can just push through, no matter how strong you are."
What are the symptoms of heat exhaustion?
The symptoms of heat exhaustion, according to the Mayo Clinic, include:
Cool, moist skin with goose bumps when in the heat
Heavy sweating
Faintness
Dizziness
Fatigue
Weak, rapid pulse
Low blood pressure when standing
Muscle cramps
Nausea
Headache
What are the symptoms of heat stroke?
The symptoms of heat stroke, according to the Mayo Clinic, include:
High body temperature
Confusion, agitation, slurred speech, irritability, delirium, seizures and coma
Skin is hot and dry
Heavy sweating if exercising
Nausea and vomiting
Skin may turn red as body temperature increases
Rapid breathing
Racing heart rate
Headache
Tips to avoid heat illness
Cleveland Clinic recommends four key things to stay safe during extreme heat:
Staying hydrated
Take frequent breaks and avoid intense exercise
Get into shade at a minimum, ideally air conditioning
